a: AWS (Amazon Web Services) is Amazon's cloud: a giant set of computers and storage you rent over the internet instead of buying your own. You only pay for what you use, and Amazon runs and maintains the hardware for you. You ask for a server or some storage and it is ready in minutes.
fs: AWS is on-demand, pay-as-you-go infrastructure you rent over the internet — Amazon owns and operates the hardware.
fj: Thinking 'the cloud' is one magic computer somewhere; it is real servers in Amazon's data centers that you rent.

q: What is a Region and an Availability Zone?
a: A Region is a physical part of the world where AWS runs data centers, like us-east-1 in Virginia or eu-west-1 in Ireland. Inside each Region are several Availability Zones (AZs) — separate data centers close together. You pick a Region near your users, and spreading across AZs keeps your app running if one data center fails.
fs: A Region is a geographic location; each holds multiple isolated Availability Zones so you can build for low latency and failover.
fj: Assuming your resources are everywhere by default; most AWS resources live in one Region you must choose.

a: EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) lets you rent a virtual server — a computer in the cloud — that you can turn on, log into, and run your programs on. You choose how much CPU and memory it has (the 'instance type'), and you pay only while it is running. It is like renting a PC by the hour.
fs: EC2 is rentable virtual servers (instances) where you control the OS and software, billed for the time they run.
fj: Leaving an instance running and assuming idle is free; you are billed while it is on, not just when busy.

a: S3 (Simple Storage Service) is AWS's place to store files — images, backups, data, anything — as 'objects' inside containers called buckets. It scales to almost any amount of data, and each file gets a web address you can use to fetch it. It stores files, but you cannot install programs or an operating system on it.
fs: S3 is durable object storage: you put/get files (objects) in buckets, addressed by a unique key, not a file system or server.
fj: Treating S3 like a hard drive you run software on; it only holds files, it does not run code.

code.label: copy a file to a bucket

a: IAM (Identity and Access Management) is how AWS controls who can do what. You create users and roles, then attach policies that say which actions they are allowed on which resources. The safe rule is 'least privilege': give each user or service only the access it truly needs.
fs: IAM manages identities (users/roles) and policies that grant or deny actions on resources, following least privilege.
fj: Granting full admin to everything 'to make it work'; that is unsafe — scope permissions to only what is needed.
